params.text:
Recognising a blocked tube

A blocked tube is one of the most common problems, and it is usually preventable. You may notice that a feed or flush will not go through, that there is unusual resistance when you try to flush, or that the pump keeps alarming. Blockages often happen when a tube is not flushed properly, when thick feed or medicine sits in it, or when medicines that should not be given down a tube have been used.

  • Feed or flush won't pass: a classic sign of a blockage.
  • Resistance or alarms: the tube feels tight or the pump keeps beeping.
  • Prevent it: flush with prescribed water before and after feeds and medicines.

What to do about a blockage

If a tube seems blocked, do not force fluid down it with a syringe and never poke anything inside the tube to clear it — this can burst the tube or push a blockage further in. Follow only the gentle steps your training and the care plan set out, such as a gentle prescribed flush, and if the tube does not clear, stop and escalate to the nurse or dietitian.

A blocked tube means the person may miss their feed, fluids and medicines, so it needs to be reported promptly rather than left. Prevention is far better than cure: consistent flushing, giving only what is prescribed, and giving medicines correctly will stop the great majority of blockages before they start. When in doubt, never restart a feed through a tube you cannot flush — escalate instead.

params.question: You try to give a water flush and it will not go through the PEG, with unusual resistance. What is the safest action?

answers.text: Do not force it, follow the gentle steps in your training, and escalate to the nurse or dietitian if it will not clear.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Never force a blocked tube; use only the method you were taught and escalate if it does not clear.

answers.text: Push hard on the syringe to force the blockage through.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — forcing fluid can burst the tube or drive the blockage further in.

answers.text: Poke a thin object down the tube to clear it.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — never put anything inside the tube; this can damage it or push the blockage in.

answers.text: Give the feed anyway and hope it clears the blockage.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No — never restart a feed through a tube you cannot flush; stop and escalate.
